title: Building With AI: Reflections on Designing a Prompt-Driven Training Platform
author: Sachiko Kijima
text:
Building With AI: Reflections on Designing a Prompt-Driven Training Platform
This is the final post in a 3-part series about building a virtual skill assessment platform powered by ChatGPT.
- Part 1: Concept— Treating ChatGPT as a runtime for skill simulations
- Part 2: Implementation — Prompt structure, file architecture, and design patterns
- Part 3: Reflections — What I learned about working with AI, and why it changed how I design
Pair Programming with ChatGPT Is Surprisingly Fun
With the VSCode plugin, I set up ChatGPT as a **real-time coding partner**. It would write Markdown or Python, and I’d review it like I would a junior teammate.
The feedback loop was fast:
- “Would this structure make sense to someone else?”
- “Can you make this part more modular?”
- “Does this prompt communicate intent clearly?”
It wasn’t always accurate — but that wasn’t the point. It helped me think through design collaboratively.
You Don’t Need a Programming Background
I realized that even non-engineers could build this kind of application.
Why? Because the interface is natural language.
Instead of writing code, I asked ChatGPT:
- “What’s the best way to express this instruction?”
- “Which version would be clearer for future readers?”
- “Can you generate a structured list that follows this logic?”
This is why I believe prompt design is UX design. You’re not just giving commands — you’re creating an experience.
TDD, Even With Prompts
Test-Driven Development (TDD) became essential.
- First I’d write the minimum prompt to make it work.
- Then I’d refactor it to improve structure, clarity, and reusability.
- Finally, I’d “test” by running it in different chats to confirm it worked as expected.
You can’t design and polish simultaneously. Separating concerns — like functionality vs communication — made everything smoother.
Managing AI Conversations as Projects
To avoid losing context, I split my conversations into dedicated threads:
- Architecture planning
- Roleplay prompt creation
- Feedback logic
- Testing and debugging
This way, each chat had a clear purpose and history. I could return anytime, and ChatGPT would “remember” the framing of that task.
Session Transitions With Trigger Keywords
Switching tasks mid-chat? I used trigger keywords to tell ChatGPT what to remember, reset, or refocus on.
Example:
“Let’s end the design discussion here. For the next session, I want to focus on feedback formatting. Please give me a keyword I can use to pick this up later.”
ChatGPT would generate a short phrase like `FEEDBACK_FORMAT_START` — and I’d use that in the new session. It worked remarkably well.
Markdown as External Memory
I used GitHub not just for storing files, but for externalizing memory for ChatGPT.
- Architecture principles → docs/principles
- Decision logs → docs/adr
- Prompt templates → shared/prompts/
These files weren’t mainly for me — they were for the assistant “AssistA”.
She (ChatGPT) could refer to them anytime to stay on track, even after switching chats.
It was like giving her a shared notebook she could peek into whenever needed.
DDD Meets Structured Prompting
The project unexpectedly mirrored Domain-Driven Design:
- I defined a shared language (e.g., “roleplay phase,” “evaluation criteria”)
- I enforced boundaries between contexts (intro vs reflection)
- I treated every part of the system — even behavior — as modular
At the same time, the prompt code itself followed structured programming principles:
- Clear flow
- No hidden state
- Explicit transitions
This hybrid approach created clarity for both the model and myself.
Final Thought: You’re Not Just Prompting — You’re Designing
This project wasn’t about writing smarter prompts.
It was about learning to design workflows, experiences, and systems in collaboration with an AI.
Treating ChatGPT as a runtime changes everything:
- You stop asking it to perform tasks.
- You start building interfaces, triggers, and expectations.
- You shift from prompt engineer to runtime architect.
And once you do that, the boundaries between software, documentation, and conversation start to dissolve — in the best possible way.
🛠️ GitHub Repository

— *Sachiko Kijima*
